refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refinement clarifies that availability is a measurable, time-based metric with explicit exclusions (planned maintenance) and inclusions (safety-driven stops). High safety criticality requires that availability measurement must not mask safety-related unavailability as acceptable downtime.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of system state transitions (operational, maintenance, fault, safety-hold) with timestamped records; post-evaluation statistical analysis of uptime ratio; cross-validation with maintenance logs and safety event records.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that prevent productive operation within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall distinguish between planned maintenance windows and unplanned downtime in availability accounting.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall implement redundancy and fault recovery mechanisms to minimize mean time to recovery (MTTR).

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement continuous health monitoring across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) with automated fault detection, logging, and alerting; couple with predictive maintenance scheduling and spare parts availability management.
DP-B: Deploy redundant critical components (dual communication links, backup power, secondary control channels) with automatic failover logic to minimize downtime duration.

DP-C: Establish a centralized availability tracking and reporting module that aggregates telemetry from all subsystems, calculates uptime metrics in real-time, and provides dashboards for stakeholders.
DP-D: Combine DP-A, DP-B, and DP-C into an integrated availability assurance strategy: continuous monitoring + redundancy + real-time visibility.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Integrated availability assurance strategy combining continuous health monitoring (DP-A), redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover (DP-B), and centralized real-time availability tracking and reporting (DP-C). This approach addresses both the prevention of failures (monitoring + redundancy) and the rapid detection and recovery from failures (failover + tracking), directly supporting the ≥0.97 availability target.

potential_sources: Health monitoring (DP-A) must integrate with Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems—moderate coupling, but well-defined via Telemetry interface.
Redundancy (DP-B) couples with Vehicle and Teleoperation design; failover logic must not conflict with Safety Services constraints—manageable via SafetyAlert interface.
Availability tracking (DP-C) depends on data from all subsystems but is logically decoupled as a consumer of Telemetry—low coupling risk.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is not orthogonal but is necessary and sufficient. The three sub-strategies (monitoring, redundancy, tracking) are complementary and address different aspects of availability. Coupling is primarily through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) rather than shared state or tight algorithmic dependencies.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant components (DP-B) are cost-effective and do not violate weight/power constraints of the Vehicle.
Assumption: Telemetry infrastructure has sufficient bandwidth and latency to support continuous health monitoring without degrading real-time control.
Risk: Failover logic (DP-B) may introduce transient unavailability during switchover; MTTR must be validated against the 0.97 target.
Risk: High safety criticality means safety-driven shutdowns must not be masked as 'availability'; measurement methodology must be transparent to Safety Manager.
Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP-A) requires historical failure data; early in system lifecycle, data may be sparse, reducing effectiveness.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) failover switchover time budget (e.g., <100ms for critical systems), (2) availability measurement exclusion criteria (e.g., safety-hold periods are logged separately, not counted as downtime), and (3) data retention and analysis frequency for health monitoring (e.g., rolling 30-day window for predictive maintenance triggers).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that orchestrates availability assurance across all subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). It owns the integrated health monitoring strategy, availability metric calculation, real-time reporting, and coordination of redundancy and failover logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health and status data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems; used for continuous monitoring and availability metric calculation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services; NGHE Solution must log these separately and exclude safety-hold periods from availability downtime accounting; may trigger coordinated shutdown or failover.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Communicates availability status and maintenance scheduling recommendations to Site Manager and Tele-operator; may include failover notifications or predictive maintenance alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication backbone for Telemetry ingestion and Tasking output; redundancy strategy (DP-B) may involve dual Network Link instances for critical control paths.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of availability assurance. It integrates monitoring, redundancy, and reporting across all subsystems. Minimal diffusion: only the actual redundant hardware (e.g., dual motors, backup power) resides in Vehicle; NGHE Solution owns the orchestration logic and availability tracking.

Vehicle could own health monitoring for its own subsystems (engine, hydraulics, sensors) but cannot own cross-system availability orchestration or failover logic. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would fragment availability assurance and create diffusion across Teleoperation and Safety Services.
Teleoperation owns its own communication link redundancy but cannot own Vehicle or Safety Services health monitoring. Availability assurance is a system-level concern, not a subsystem concern.

Safety Services owns safety-critical monitoring and alerts but must not own overall availability assurance; doing so would conflate safety constraints with availability metrics, violating the requirement to measure availability transparently.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate owner because: (1) availability is a system-level KPI that spans all subsystems, (2) the integrated strategy (monitoring + redundancy + reporting) requires cross-subsystem orchestration, (3) NGHE Solution already owns the top-level integration and stakeholder interfaces (Tasking to Site Manager), and (4) this placement minimizes diffusion and maintains clear separation of concerns (Vehicle owns its hardware, Safety Services owns safety logic, NGHE Solution owns availability assurance).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it accumulates too many cross-cutting concerns. Mitigation: define clear, narrow responsibilities (availability tracking and orchestration only; delegate actual health monitoring algorithms to subsystems via Telemetry interface).

Risk: Redundancy logic (DP-B) may require tight coupling with Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops. Mitigation: define failover triggers and switchover protocols as part of the DP-D specification; use SafetyAlert and Telemetry interfaces to decouple logic from implementation.

Risk: Real-time availability reporting (DP-C) may introduce latency or computational overhead. Mitigation: implement availability metric calculation asynchronously; use rolling windows and batch updates to avoid real-time bottlenecks.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the NGHE Solution's internal architecture for DP-D: (1) a Health Monitoring Coordinator that subscribes to Telemetry and aggregates subsystem health, (2) a Failover Orchestrator that manages redundancy and switchover logic, (3) an Availability Metrics Engine that calculates uptime ratios and logs safety-driven shutdowns separately, and (4) a Reporting Service that publishes availability status via Tasking. This internal decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries and reduces the risk of diffusion.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (integrated availability assurance) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The three sub-strategies (continuous monitoring, redundancy, real-time tracking) are necessary and sufficient to meet the KPI. Coupling is acceptable and managed via well-defined interfaces. Assumptions and risks are documented and mitigatable.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Redundancy logic (DP-B) may require Vehicle and Teleoperation to implement failover-aware control loops. Mitigation: define failover triggers and switchover protocols as part of the DP-D specification; use SafetyAlert interface to coordinate safety-critical failovers.
Low risk: Health monitoring (DP-A) is primarily a data consumer (Telemetry); subsystems own their own health sensors and algorithms. NGHE Solution owns aggregation and analysis.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the clear owner with a containment score of 0.95. The module boundary is well-defined: NGHE Solution owns availability orchestration and reporting; Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services own their own health monitoring and provide data via Telemetry. Redundancy hardware (e.g., dual motors) resides in Vehicle, but failover logic resides in NGHE Solution. This separation is clean and minimizes diffusion.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Availability is measurable via continuous telemetry logging and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all subsystems to report state transitions (operational, fault, maintenance, safety-hold), (2) timestamped logging of these transitions, (3) post-evaluation calculation of uptime ratio, and (4) cross-validation with maintenance logs and safety event records. All of these are technically feasible and align with the Telemetry interface.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution, which owns the integrated availability assurance strategy. Sub-FRs (fault detection, maintenance accounting, real-time visibility, redundancy) are allocated to specific sub-strategies within DP-D and will be further decomposed in the next level. Stakeholder responsibilities are clear: Site Manager and Tele-operator receive availability status via Tasking; Safety Manager reviews safety-hold logs separately; Maintenance/Quality uses predictive maintenance alerts.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to include explicit failover switchover time budgets (e.g., <100ms for critical systems) and validate that switchover time does not violate the 0.97 availability target.

Define availability measurement methodology in detail: (1) what constitutes 'operational' vs. 'unavailable', (2) how safety-driven shutdowns are logged and excluded from downtime, (3) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days), and (4) the confidence interval for the 0.97 target.

Clarify the role of predictive maintenance (DP-A) in the early lifecycle when historical failure data is sparse; consider a phased approach (reactive maintenance initially, transitioning to predictive as data accumulates).

Define the internal architecture of NGHE Solution (Health Monitoring Coordinator, Failover Orchestrator, Availability Metrics Engine, Reporting Service) to clarify ownership and reduce diffusion risk.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined, the selected DP is sound and addresses the FR, and the assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is appropriate with high containment and low diffusion. However, the FR is not atomic—it decomposes into four sub-FRs (fault detection, maintenance accounting, real-time visibility, redundancy). The DP-D strategy is complex and requires further decomposition to clarify the internal architecture of NGHE Solution and the allocation of sub-strategies to specific modules or components. Proceeding to the next level of decomposition is necessary to validate the feasibility of the integrated strategy and to allocate responsibilities to subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
decision.justification_detail: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that cannot be fully addressed at a single level of decomposition. The refined FR explicitly identifies four sub-FRs, and DP-D (integrated strategy) requires further decomposition to clarify: (1) how fault detection is implemented across subsystems, (2) how maintenance windows are distinguished from unplanned downtime, (3) how real-time availability visibility is provided to stakeholders, and (4) how redundancy and failover logic are orchestrated. The next level of decomposition will allocate these sub-strategies to specific modules and clarify the interfaces and dependencies.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The system shall detect and log all fault conditions that prevent productive operation within 5 seconds.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The system shall distinguish between planned maintenance windows and unplanned downtime in availability accounting.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The system shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The system shall implement redundancy and fault recovery mechanisms to minimize mean time to recovery (MTTR).

assumptions: Redundant components (DP-B) are cost-effective and do not violate weight/power constraints of the Vehicle.
Telemetry infrastructure has sufficient bandwidth and latency to support continuous health monitoring without degrading real-time control.
Availability measurement excludes planned maintenance windows and safety-driven shutdowns; these are logged separately and not counted as downtime.
Historical failure data will become available as the system operates; predictive maintenance effectiveness will improve over time.
Failover switchover time is <100ms for critical systems, allowing the system to maintain the 0.97 availability target even with transient unavailability during switchover.
open_risks: Failover logic may introduce transient unavailability during switchover; MTTR must be validated against the 0.97 target.
High safety criticality means safety-driven shutdowns must not be masked as 'availability'; measurement methodology must be transparent to Safety Manager.
Predictive maintenance (DP-A) requires historical failure data; early in system lifecycle, data may be sparse, reducing effectiveness.
NGHE Solution may become a 'god module' if it accumulates too many cross-cutting concerns; clear responsibility boundaries must be maintained.
Redundancy logic (DP-B) may require tight coupling with Vehicle and Teleoperation control loops; failover triggers and switchover protocols must be carefully designed.
evidence_needed: Detailed failover switchover time measurements and validation against the 0.97 availability target.
Availability measurement methodology document: definition of 'operational' vs. 'unavailable', handling of safety-driven shutdowns, evaluation period, confidence interval.
Telemetry bandwidth and latency analysis to confirm that continuous health monitoring does not degrade real-time control.
Redundancy cost-benefit analysis: cost of redundant components vs. improvement in availability.
Predictive maintenance algorithm design and validation plan; phased approach for early lifecycle when historical data is sparse.
Internal architecture design for NGHE Solution: Health Monitoring Coordinator, Failover Orchestrator, Availability Metrics Engine, Reporting Service.
Failover trigger and switchover protocol specifications; integration with SafetyAlert interface.
Post-evaluation availability analysis: uptime ratio calculation, cross-validation with maintenance logs and safety event records.
